summary: Charity Majors reflects on how Twitter was essential to both Honeycomb's growth and her personal creative process, serving as a dopamine source for her undiagnosed ADHD. With social media fragmented and her old writing workflow broken, she commits to a three-month experiment of writing shorter, faster blog posts weekly — learning to ship without the crutch of Twitter threads or deadline-driven panic.
key_points: Twitter conversations directly shaped Honeycomb's product strategy by revealing observability was an 'everybody problem'
Undiagnosed ADHD drove unhealthy coping mechanisms including sleep deprivation and Twitter-fueled dopamine hits
The fragmentation of social media has destroyed the combative tech discourse she thrived in
Epic Twitter threads that took enormous effort have vanished, while blog posts endured
She's committing to writing one sub-1000-word blog post per week to relearn scoping and shipping
money_quotes: In retrospect, I now realize that I was doing things like 'outbounding' and 'product marketing' and 'category creation', but at the time it felt more like oxygen.
I picked fights, I argued. I was combative and aggressive in public, and I loved it. I regret some of it now; I burned some good relationships, and I burned out my adrenal glands.
Most of those epic threads I put so much time and energy into crafting have vanished into the ether. The few that I bothered to convert into essay format are the only ones that have endured.
For most of my adult life, I've relied on hard deadlines and panic to drive projects to completion, or to determine the scope of a piece.

When I look back over things I have written, I feel like I can see myself growing up, my mental health improving, I'm getting better at taking the long view, being more empathetic, being less reactive.
key_insight: The writing that endures is the writing you ship in durable form, not the writing that feeds the dopamine cycle of social media — and learning to ship without those crutches requires deliberately embracing imperfection.
